title: Least-Privilege Validation — Interactive Logons to Database Tier from Workstations
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the PCI DSS programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with PCI DSS control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 8.2.1 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 8.2.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.43: Least-Privilege Validation — Interactive Logons to Database Tier from Workstations.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.11.43' running on index=db mssql:audit action="AUDIT SESSION" or sourcetype="oracle:audit"; lookup pci_jump_hosts.csv client IP, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Least-Privilege Validation — Interactive Logons to Database ' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper PCI DSS control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the PCI DSS control objective.

dataSources: `index=db` `mssql:audit` action="AUDIT SESSION" or `sourcetype="oracle:audit"`; `lookup pci_jump_hosts.csv` client IP
app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft SQL Server (2648) if audit ingested (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=db sourcetype="mssql:audit" earliest=-24h
| where match(action,"(?i)login|authentication")
| lookup pci_jump_hosts.csv ip AS client_ip OUTPUT is_jump
| where isnull(is_jump) OR is_jump="false"
| lookup pci_db_tier.csv server_name OUTPUT tier
| where tier="carddata_db"
| stats count by client_ip, server_name, database_principal_name
| sort - count
description: Surfaces database interactive logins from non-administrator workstations that may violate least privilege for personnel access to cardholder data stores.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for PCI DSS compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes db and sourcetypes mssql:audit, oracle:audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Populate jump host egress IPs. (2) Exclude application service accounts via lookup. (3) Pair with bastion session logs for false positives. (4) Quarterly access review input. Indexes required: `index=db`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=mssql:audit`, `sourcetype=oracle:audit`.
visualization: Table (client_ip, principal), geomap, bar (count by server_name).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection.Intrusion_Detection by Intrusion_Detection.signature, Intrusion_Detection.dest, Intrusion_Detection.severity | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Intrusion_Detection (CIM data model).

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft SQL Server

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised penetration testing** — Annual or more frequent penetration tests conducted by QSA-approved assessors against cardholder data environment (CDE) systems produce expected security alerts that are pre-communicated and tracked via the test engagement letter.

2. **PCI ASV scanning activity** — Quarterly external vulnerability scans performed by Approved Scanning Vendors generate network probe events that trigger IDS/IPS alerts as an expected part of the PCI DSS validation process.

3. **Tokenization system batch processing** — The payment tokenization platform performs scheduled batch re-tokenization and token lifecycle management operations that produce high-volume cardholder data access patterns within approved operational windows.

4. **Acquirer and processor settlement activity** — End-of-day settlement reconciliation between the merchant platform and payment processor involves legitimate PAN/SAD data transmission that is covered by the processor service provider agreement.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `pci_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved pen-test engagement IDs, ASV scan source IPs, scheduled batch job names, and settlement processing windows. The search filters matches where `pci_exception_valid=true` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk DB Connect + Microsoft SQL Audit Export (Splunkbase app ID 2686) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk DB Connect + Microsoft SQL Audit Export" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `db` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `pci_jump_hosts.csv`, `pci_db_tier.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('pci_jump_hosts.csv','pci_db_tier.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `8.2.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI DSS 8.2.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.43: Least-Privilege Validation — Interactive Logons to Database Tier from Workstations." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk DB Connect + Microsoft SQL Audit Export (Splunkbase app ID 2686), landing events in `db` under `mssql:audit`. MSSQL audit trail on a transactional DB generates 1000-10000 events/instance/hour depending on audit spec (~1-10 GB/instance/month).

Configure the input through Splunk DB Connect > Inputs > New Rising Column Input against server_audits view. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[mi_input://mssql_audit_daily]
connection = mssql_prod
query = SELECT * FROM sys.fn_get_audit_file(...) WHERE event_time > ?
index = mssql
sourcetype = mssql:audit
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `session_server_principal_name, object_name, statement, action_class, application_name, database_name, event_time`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=db earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Populate jump host egress IPs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Exclude application service accounts via lookup. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 3:** Pair with bastion session logs for false positives. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Quarterly access review input. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Least-Privilege Validation — Interactive Logons to Database Tier from Workstations," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `pci_jump_hosts.csv` — the authoritative Cardholder Data Environment (CDE) scope register. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`is_jump`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `ip`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `pci_jump_hosts.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval ip = lower(ip)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 24 hours.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Intrusion_Detection` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection.Intrusion_Detection by Intrusion_Detection.signature, Intrusion_Detection.dest, Intrusion_Detection.severity | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Intrusion_Detection` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.11.43: Least-Privilege Validation — Interactive Logons to Database ]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.11.43,reg=PCI_DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=db sourcetype=mssql:audit earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `client_ip`, `database_principal_name`, `server_name`. Run:

```spl
index=db sourcetype=mssql:audit earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table client_ip, database_principal_name, server_name
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `pci_jump_hosts.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup pci_jump_hosts.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.11.43: PCI DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`client_ip`, `principal`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Geomap (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Bar (`count by server_name`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We surface database interactive logins from non-administrator workstations that may violate least privilege for personnel access to cardholder data stores. PCI DSS is about protecting card and payment data when we run these checks.
